turns.user_message: Look, I know I smoke a lot. It's just something I do. I don't really see the big deal. My friends and family all smoke, so it's just a normal part of my life.
step2_rag_chunks: Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.
Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.
Quitting smoking reduces the risk of lung disease, heart disease, and many types of cancer.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the ASSESSMENT phase. Focus on gathering basic facts: how long they have been smoking, how much they smoke per day, and what substance. Do NOT ask about triggers or suggest strategies yet. Ask only one simple factual question.

- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal

step2_rag_chunks: Nicotine addiction is both physical and psychological. Addressing both aspects can improve success in quitting.

If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
Quitting smoking reduces the risk of lung disease, heart disease, and many types of cancer.

relevant_strategies.strategy: quit a couple of times before

Therapist: I understand that it's a big part of your social life, but I'm concerned about the impact it's having on your health. Can you tell me more about what you mean by "it's just something I do"? What does smoking mean to you?

Patient: It's just something I've always done. I started in college, and it's just become a habit. I don't really think about it that much. And like I said, all my friends do it, so it's not like I'm going to quit just because you're telling me to.

Therapist: I'm not here to tell you what to do, Alex. I'm here to help you understand the risks and benefits of quitting. But I sense that you're feeling a bit resistant to the idea of quitting. Can you tell me more about what's holding you back?

Patient: You don't understand. If I quit, my friends will think I'm lame or something. They'll stop inviting me to hang out. And what am I supposed to do instead of smoking when we're out at the bar? Just stand there and do nothing?

Therapist: I think I do understand, Alex. You're worried about losing your social connections if you quit. That's a tough spot to be in. But can I ask you, have you ever thought about what your life would be like if you didn't have to worry about smoking all the time? If you could find other ways to cope with stress and anxiety?

Patient: I guess not, really. I mean, I've tried to quit a couple of times before, but it didn't last. And yeah, it would be nice not to have to worry about finding a place to smoke or having to leave the bar to go outside. But I just don't know if it's worth it.
